title: Gowers Uniformity of Arithmetic Functions in Short Intervals
speaker: Joni Teräväinen
affiliation: Von Neumann Fellow, School of Mathematics
abstract: I will present results establishing cancellation in short sums of arithmetic functions (in particular the von Mangoldt and divisor functions) twisted by polynomial exponential phases, or more general nilsequence phases. These results imply the Gowers uniformity of suitably normalized versions of these functions in intervals of length $X^{c}$ around $X$ for suitable values of $c$ (depending on the function and on whether one considers all or almost all short sums). I will also discuss applications to averaged forms of the Hardy-Littlewood conjecture.
tags: Workshop on Dynamics, Discrete Analysis and Multiplicative Number Theory

proof_stages.label: Model-subtracted exponential-sum formulation
proof_stages.summary: The talk selects the Möbius, von Mangoldt, and divisor functions, formulates short polynomial exponential-sum cancellation as the U^2 precursor to Gowers uniformity, and introduces model functions so that major-arc main terms are removed uniformly.

proof_stages.label: Short-interval exponential and nilsequence estimates
proof_stages.summary: After benchmarking previous all-interval and almost-all-interval exponents, the main theorems give cancellation for f minus its model in short intervals, with the same exponent ranges extending to bounded-complexity nilsequence twists.

proof_stages.label: Inverse-theorem transfer and applications
proof_stages.summary: The Green-Tao-Ziegler inverse theorem converts the nilsequence bounds into short-interval Gowers-uniformity estimates, which then yield applications to prime linear patterns, ergodic averages, and averaged Chowla, Hardy-Littlewood, and divisor-correlation statements.

proof_stages.label: Prime proof reduction to bilinear types
proof_stages.summary: For the proof model case of the von Mangoldt function in almost all intervals of length x^{1/3+epsilon}, a prime identity decomposes the relevant sums into Type I, Type I_2, and Type II ranges over products of variables.

proof_stages.label: Type I and Type I_2 estimates
proof_stages.summary: A long variable gives Type I cancellation unless the phase is major-arc, already neutralized by the model, while the two-variable hyperbola case is partitioned into arithmetic progressions, producing the x^{1/3} threshold.

proof_stages.label: Arithmetic Type II case
proof_stages.summary: When the polynomial phase locally behaves like n^{it}, Taylor expansion and multiplicativity allow Vinogradov-style Type I/Type II estimates to control the bilinear sum.

proof_stages.label: Non-Archimedean Type II functional equation
proof_stages.summary: In the remaining Type II case, large bilinear correlations force, through density and large-sieve arguments, an approximate functional equation for the worst frequency alpha(x), whose only solutions are essentially t/x^d and hence reduce back to the arithmetic case.

proof_stages.label: Range covering and conclusion
proof_stages.summary: The Type I, Type I_2, and Type II estimates cover all possible variable-size vectors, with the case of three variables of size x^{1/3} explaining the limiting obstruction, and analogous decompositions give the other arithmetic-function results.

examples.name: Constant-polynomial phase as major-arc obstruction
examples.note: The constant phase shows concretely why one cannot expect cancellation without treating or subtracting major-arc main terms, and it reappears as the degree-zero benchmark in the survey of known exponents.
examples.engagement_reason: The constant polynomial phase is used as a simple major-arc obstruction where cancellation can fail, but no calculation is carried out for that specific phase.

passes.reason: The degree-zero phase is a collapsed polynomial case, used to show cancellation fails on major arcs without subtracting a model.
The constant phase is the degree-zero, translation-invariant extreme major-arc case used to show cancellation can fail.
a degree-zero phase has no oscillation and is used to show cancellation can fail without treating major arcs
The constant phase is a translation-invariant, non-oscillatory degree-zero polynomial and is cited to show cancellation fails without excluding major arcs.
the constant polynomial is the degree-zero nonoscillatory phase, used to show cancellation can fail without excluding major arcs
A constant phase is a degenerate, translation-invariant polynomial phase used to show cancellation can fail on major arcs.
The degree-zero phase is translation-invariant and non-oscillatory, and is used to show cancellation fails without handling major arcs.
The constant phase has zero oscillation, the most collapsed/major-arc case, and is used to show cancellation cannot hold without excluding such phases.
A constant phase has no oscillation and is used to show cancellation fails without a major-arc exception.
It is used to show uniform cancellation cannot hold for all phases, and the constant phase is a degree-zero maximally regular degeneration.
A degree-zero phase is translation-invariant and minimally oscillatory, and is cited as a major-arc case where cancellation fails.
The constant phase is a zero-degree, translation-invariant no-oscillation boundary case used to show cancellation can fail on major arcs.

examples.name: Short-interval prime pattern with two linear constraints
examples.note: The system requiring primes in a short interval with relations such as p1+p2+p3=n and 2p1-p2 also prime illustrates the linear-equations-in-primes application of the Gowers-uniformity theorem.
examples.engagement_reason: The two-constraint prime pattern is given as an example of an application finding solutions in short intervals, without working through a concrete instance or local checks.

passes.reason: The two-constraint short-interval prime system is an ordinary linear-pattern application rather than an obstruction.
The two-constraint short-interval prime pattern is an ordinary admissible linear pattern used as an application, not to refute a claim.
it is an ordinary finite system of linear prime constraints used as an illustrative application
This is an ordinary admissible system of linear prime constraints in short intervals, used as an application rather than as a failure case.
the two-constraint prime pattern is an ordinary finite system of linear forms used as an application of the theorem
The displayed two-constraint prime configuration is an ordinary linear prime pattern used as an application, not to refute a claim.

The two-constraint short-interval prime pattern is a representative admissible linear-pattern application rather than an obstruction.
It is an ordinary illustrative system of linear prime constraints to which the theorem applies.
It serves as an illustrative admissible linear prime pattern in short intervals, with no sporadic, optimal, or collapsed structure.
It is an illustrative ordinary system of linear prime constraints in a short interval, not used to refute anything.
This is an ordinary short-interval linear prime pattern used as a positive application of the theorem.

examples.name: The obstruction to both n and n+1 being prime
examples.note: In response to a question about positivity of the Hardy-Littlewood constant, the adjacent-prime obstruction illustrates the role of local congruence conditions.
examples.engagement_reason: The impossibility of both n and n+1 being prime is cited as a local obstruction example, with no explicit verification beyond the statement.

passes.reason: The consecutive-prime tuple has a zero local/singular-series obstruction modulo 2, and is used to show positivity can fail without local solubility.
The adjacent-prime pattern has the minimal gap and a mod-2 obstruction collapsing the expected prime-pair count, so it is used as a failure case for positivity without local conditions.
the adjacent prime pattern is the minimal-gap case and is cited as a local obstruction to simultaneous primality
The tuple n,n+1 has a mod-2 local obstruction, so its prime-pattern count collapses, and it is used as the inadmissible case.
the pattern n,n+1 has the minimal possible positive gap and is cited as a local parity obstruction to simultaneous primality
The pattern n,n+1 is locally obstructed modulo 2, giving a collapsed prime-tuple case used as an obstruction example.
The pattern n,n+1 is the minimal-gap prime-pair boundary case and is cited as a local parity obstruction.
The adjacent pair n,n+1 is the minimum-gap prime-pair pattern and its mod-2 obstruction makes the expected prime pattern collapse, illustrating failure of local admissibility.
The adjacent prime pair is the minimal-gap two-form pattern with a parity obstruction, used to show local conditions are necessary.
The pattern n,n+1 is used as the mod-2 local obstruction, with minimal prime gap and a collapsed admissible residue set.
The adjacent-prime pattern has the minimal possible gap and collapses under the mod-2 obstruction, illustrating failure of admissibility.
The pattern n,n+1 is the minimal-gap prime-pair case whose solution set collapses by the parity obstruction.

examples.name: Dirichlet divisor-problem hyperbola analogy
examples.note: The classical problem of approximating lattice points under a hyperbola by lines is mentioned as an analogy for the Type I_2 partition into arithmetic progressions, not as a required input.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker reasons through the hyperbola setup by partitioning lattice points under a hyperbola into arithmetic progressions using slope approximations.

passes.reason: The divisor-problem hyperbola is a standard representative analogy for lattice-point decomposition, not a special exception or adversarial case.

examples.name: Fourier coefficients of modular forms as a limitation test
examples.note: An audience question about modular-form coefficients clarifies that multiplicativity alone is insufficient for the methods, since the needed prime-value information and higher correlations are not comparably understood.
examples.engagement_reason: Fourier coefficients of modular forms are discussed as a possible limitation of the methods, but no computation or concrete construction is performed on them.

passes.reason: Modular-form Fourier coefficients are a normal arithmetic-function test case raised to expose a limitation of the methods.
Fourier coefficients of modular forms are a normal automorphic arithmetic function, but they are raised as a case where the stated methods do not presently extend.
